children.value:
In chapter 3 we built up a toolkit of polynomial functions by combining power
functions. Polynomials are very flexible, but they cannot describe one of the
most common behaviors in engineering: processes that grow or decay at a rate
proportional to their current value. A capacitor charging through a resistor, a
hot component cooling down, a vibration losing energy to friction: all of these
follow the same mathematical pattern, and none of them can be captured by a
polynomial. To describe them we need the exponential function.
You know the definition of the

children.html: f(x)=axf(x) = a^xf(x)=ax
a>0a > 0a>0
a≠1a \neq 1a=1
eee
f(x)=exf(x) = e^xf(x)=ex

children.value:
You know the key properties of the exponential function: domain,
range, zeros, monotonicity, boundedness, and symmetry.
You can sketch the graph of the exponential function for different bases.

for exponential functions and can
apply them.

The reverse process, discharging a fully charged capacitor through a resistor,
gives the curve:

(Source: own figure; license

These curves appear throughout engineering: the temperature of a cooling
component, the amplitude of a damped oscillation, the concentration of a
substance undergoing radioactive decay, and the speed of a vehicle braking
against air resistance all follow the same exponential pattern. Understanding
the exponential function is therefore not optional for an engineer.
Video “Introduction to Exponential Functions” by Nerdstudy
The general form of the exponential function is

The key properties of the exponential function follow directly from its
definition. They are worth comparing with the polynomial properties we studied
in chapter 3.

children.html: R\mathbb{R}R

children.value:
: we can raise a
positive number to any real power. The range is

children.html: (0,∞)(0, \infty)(0,∞)

children.value:
: the
exponential function is always strictly positive. In particular,

children.html: ax=0a^x = 0ax=0

children.value:
has
no solution, so the exponential function has no zeros.

children.html: (0,1)(0, 1)(0,1)
a0=1a^0 = 1a0=1
aaa

children.value:
, every exponential
function passes through the point

children.html: (0,1)(0, 1)(0,1)
a>1a > 1a>1

children.value:
, the function is strictly monotonically
increasing: larger exponents give larger values. If

children.html: 0<a<10 < a < 10<a<1

children.value:
, the function
is strictly monotonically decreasing: larger exponents give smaller values. The
charging curve uses

children.html: a=e>1a = e > 1a=e>1

children.value:
(increasing), while the discharging curve uses
the same base but with a negative exponent, which is equivalent to using a base
less than 1 (decreasing).

children.html: a>1a > 1a>1

children.value:
the function is bounded below by 0 but unbounded
above: as

children.html: x→+∞x \to +\inftyx→+∞

children.value: the function grows without bound. For

children.html: 0<a<10 < a < 10<a<1

children.value: the function is also bounded below by 0 but unbounded above: as

children.html: x→−∞x \to -\inftyx→−∞

children.value:
the function grows without bound. In both cases the value 0 is never reached,
since

children.html: ax>0a^x > 0ax>0

children.title: 07 - What is an Exponential Function? (Exponential Growth, Decay & Graphing).

children.value: Euler’s number and the natural exponential function

children.label: Euler’s number and the natural exponential function

children.value:
Among all possible bases, one stands out as the natural choice for science and
engineering:

children.html: e=2.71828 18284 59045 …e = 2.71828\,18284\,59045\,\ldotse=2.718281828459045…
π\piπ

children.value:
, and it appears in an enormous range of
mathematical and physical contexts. Its defining property, which we will
appreciate fully when we study differentiation, is the following: the function

children.html: f(x)=exf(x) = e^xf(x)=ex

children.value:
is the only exponential function whose rate of change at every
point equals its own value at that point. In other words, the slope of the
graph of

children.html: exe^xex
xxx
exe^xex

children.value:
. No other base has this
property.

children.html: exe^xex

children.value:
the natural building block for
describing processes where the rate of change is proportional to the current
value, which is precisely the situation in our RC circuit: the charging current
is proportional to the remaining voltage difference, and the result is the
exponential charging curve.
What is ... the natural exponential function?

children.html: f(x)=exf(x) = e^xf(x)=ex
e≈2.71828e \approx 2.71828e≈2.71828

children.value:
is Euler’s number. It is the standard exponential function
used in science and engineering.
In the RC circuit, the time constant

children.html: τ\tauτ
e−t/τe^{-t/\tau}e−t/τ

children.value: . This is the natural exponential function evaluated at

children.html: −t/τ-t/\tau−t/τ

g(t)=−t/τg(t) = -t/\taug(t)=−t/τ
f(u)=euf(u) = e^uf(u)=eu

children.value:
. We will need this
observation when we differentiate such expressions using the chain rule.

children.title: What's so special about Euler's number e? | Chapter 5, Essence of calculus

children.value:
Working with exponential expressions requires a set of algebraic rules.
These rules follow directly from the definition of exponentiation and hold for
any base

children.html: a>0a > 0a>0
a≠1a \neq 1a=1

children.value:
When multiplying two exponentials with the same base, we add
the exponents:

children.html: am⋅an=am+n.a^m \cdot a^n = a^{m+n}.am⋅an=am+n.
e−t/τ⋅e−s/τ=e−(t+s)/τe^{-t/\tau} \cdot e^{-s/\tau} = e^{-(t+s)/\tau}e−t/τ⋅e−s/τ=e−(t+s)/τ

children.value:
When dividing two exponentials with the same base, we
subtract the exponents:

children.html: aman=am−n.\frac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}.anam​=am−n.

children.value:
When raising an exponential to a further power, we multiply
the exponents:

children.html: (am)n=am⋅n.(a^m)^n = a^{m \cdot n}.(am)n=am⋅n.

children.value: A negative exponent gives the reciprocal:

children.html: a−n=1an.a^{-n} = \frac{1}{a^n}.a−n=an1​.

children.value: This is why the discharging curve

children.html: e−t/τe^{-t/\tau}e−t/τ
ttt
−t/τ-t/\tau−t/τ

children.value: becomes more and more negative, and

children.html: e−t/τ=1/et/τe^{-t/\tau} = 1/e^{t/\tau}e−t/τ=1/et/τ

children.value:
The rules above apply when the bases are the same. To
convert between different bases, we use the identity:

children.html: ax=exln⁡a,a^x = e^{x \ln a},ax=exlna,
ln⁡\lnln

children.value: is the natural logarithm, which we will introduce in section 4.3.

The exponential function describes processes where a quantity grows or decays
at a rate proportional to its current value. Its key features are a domain of
all real numbers, a strictly positive range, no zeros, and monotonic
behavior controlled by the base. Among all exponential functions,

children.html: exe^xex

children.value:
is the
natural choice for science and engineering because of its unique property in
differentiation. The calculation rules allow us to simplify and manipulate
exponential expressions algebraically. The capacitor charging curve raises a
natural question: if we know the voltage and want to find the time at which it
was reached, we need to solve an equation of the form

children.html: e−t/τ=ce^{-t/\tau} = ce−t/τ=c
ttt

children.value:
. This requires a function that undoes the exponential, and building that
function is the subject of the next section.
